content.text: The Attribution Problem for Champion Networks

Champion networks are effective precisely because their influence is informal and relational. A peer asks a champion a question over coffee; three days later they successfully use GenAI to cut an analysis task from four hours to forty minutes. How much of that outcome is attributable to the champion interaction? How do you prove it to an executive sponsor who controls the programme budget?
You cannot attribute every outcome to every champion interaction. But you can build a measurement system that makes champion contribution
visible, credible, and actionable
— visible enough that the network's value is undeniable, credible enough that it survives audit, actionable enough that it identifies where the network is and is not working.

This document presents the five-layer attribution funnel, the interaction log that makes attribution defensible, the quarterly impact report structure, and the Python models for computing and presenting impact data.
The Five-Layer Attribution Funnel

The funnel tracks a peer's journey from first champion contact to measurable business outcome. Each layer requires a different measurement instrument. The conversion rates shown are illustrative — your baseline will differ, but the funnel shape (widest at reach, narrowest at outcome) is structural and consistent.
Layer 1: Reach — Who Has the Network Touched?

Reach is the count of unique peers who had at least one meaningful interaction with a champion in the measurement period. Meaningful excludes accidental (e.g. a champion happened to be in a meeting where GenAI was discussed); it means a peer knowingly engaged with a champion in their champion capacity.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

InteractionType = str  # "office_hours" | "workshop" | "async_qa" | "use_case_consult" | "buddy_session"

@dataclass
class ChampionInteraction:
    """A single logged interaction between a champion and a peer."""
    interaction_id: str
    champion_name: str
    peer_name: str
    peer_role: str
    peer_business_unit: str
    interaction_date: date
    interaction_type: InteractionType
    topic: str                          # what the peer asked / what the session covered
    outcome_note: str                   # what the peer said they would try; what was resolved
    logged_within_24h: bool             # was this logged same day or next day?
    follow_up_outcome: Optional[str] = None  # filled in at follow-up survey (30 days later)

    def is_attributable(self) -> bool:
        """An interaction is attributable only if it was logged promptly with a concrete outcome note."""
        return (
            self.logged_within_24h and
            len(self.outcome_note.strip()) > 20 and  # not a blank or trivial note
            self.topic.strip() != ""
        )

interaction = ChampionInteraction(
    interaction_id="INT-2025-0412-001",
    champion_name="Jordan Lee",
    peer_name="Alex Patel",
    peer_role="Financial Analyst",
    peer_business_unit="Finance Operations",
    interaction_date=date(2025, 4, 12),
    interaction_type="async_qa",
    topic="Whether Copilot can summarise a lengthy contract for key dates and obligations",
    outcome_note=(
        "Explained how to structure the extraction prompt; shared the 'contract key terms' "
        "template from the repository. Alex will try it on a non-sensitive internal contract first "
        "and report back."
    ),
    logged_within_24h=True,
)
print("Attributable:", interaction.is_attributable())  # True
The
logged_within_24h
flag is not bureaucratic pedantry. Memory of what was discussed degrades rapidly — a note written a week later is reconstructed, not recorded. The interaction log's credibility depends on recency.
Layer 2: Engagement — Did the Peer Act on It?

Reach tells you the network touched someone. Engagement tells you the interaction produced action. Measure engagement via a 30-day follow-up pulse — a two-question message sent to every peer in the reach pool:
Did you try the approach / tool / prompt that was discussed? (Yes / No / Partially)
If yes, what was the result? (one-sentence free text)
Do not make this a long survey. Two questions get high response rates. Three or more get abandoned.
Layer 3: Skill Change — Is the Peer Getting Better?
Skill change is the most difficult layer to measure without creating an assessment burden that peers resist. Use three lightweight signals:
Signal
Measurement Method
Threshold for 'Skill Change'
Prompt quality improvement
Compare prompts submitted to the repository by the same peer before and after champion interaction; scored by peer reviewer on a 1-5 rubric
Score improvement of >= 1 point on the 5-point rubric
Self-assessed confidence
Single 1-5 question in the 30-day follow-up: 'How confident are you using GenAI tools for your core tasks?'
Score >= 4, or improvement from < 3 to >= 3
Reduced escalation rate
Peers who initially asked the champion basic questions (tool access, what is a prompt) and now ask more advanced questions (chaining, evaluation)
Qualitative shift observed and noted by champion in interaction log
Layer 4: Behaviour Change — Is GenAI Embedded in Daily Work?

Behaviour change is the most important layer for the programme team and the hardest to observe directly. The most reliable instrument is the 90-day self-report, combined with manager corroboration for a sample of peers.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

BehaviourChangeStatus = Literal[
    "embedded",      # uses GenAI tools daily or near-daily as a standard part of workflow
    "occasional",    # uses GenAI tools a few times per week for specific tasks
    "experimental",  # still trying tools; not yet part of regular workflow
    "not_adopted",   # has not adopted GenAI tools despite interaction
]

@dataclass
class BehaviourChangeRecord:
    peer_name: str
    days_since_first_interaction: int
    status: BehaviourChangeStatus
    primary_use_case: str           # how they are using GenAI in their role
    manager_corroborated: bool      # has the manager confirmed the self-report?

    def counts_as_behaviour_change(self) -> bool:
        return self.status in ("embedded", "occasional")

    def attribution_strength(self) -> str:
        if self.counts_as_behaviour_change() and self.manager_corroborated:
            return "STRONG"    # both self-report and manager confirmation
        if self.counts_as_behaviour_change():
            return "MODERATE"  # self-report only
        return "NONE"

record = BehaviourChangeRecord(
    peer_name="Alex Patel",
    days_since_first_interaction=90,
    status="embedded",
    primary_use_case=(
        "Uses the contract extraction prompt template daily for first-pass contract review; "
        "uses Copilot for variance commentary drafts each month-end close"
    ),
    manager_corroborated=True,
)
print(record.attribution_strength())  # STRONG
Layer 5: Business Outcome — What Did It Change for the Organisation?
Business outcomes are the funnel's narrowest layer and the one executive sponsors care about most. Three outcome types are trackable:

Time savings: documented reduction in time spent on a specific task, before and after GenAI adoption. Requires a pre-adoption baseline (ask the peer to estimate time per task at the first interaction).
Use case deployment: a production use case where champion involvement is documented in the interaction log at the time of the engagement — not retroactively attributed.
Error / rework reduction: documented reduction in revision cycles, returned documents, or quality failures in AI-assisted tasks vs manual baseline.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional, Literal

OutcomeType = Literal["time_saving", "use_case_deployed", "error_reduction", "revenue_impact"]

@dataclass
class ChampionBusinessOutcome:
    outcome_id: str
    outcome_type: OutcomeType
    peer_name: str
    champion_name: str
    # Supporting interaction log IDs (must exist and be attributable)
    interaction_ids: list[str]
    description: str
    # Quantification (at least one must be populated)
    hours_saved_per_month: Optional[float] = None
    annualised_hours_saved: Optional[float] = None
    use_case_id: Optional[str] = None         # references the use case pipeline
    error_rate_before: Optional[float] = None  # percentage
    error_rate_after: Optional[float] = None

    def is_attributable(self, interaction_log: dict) -> tuple[bool, str]:
        """Verify that all cited interactions exist and are attributable."""
        missing = [i for i in self.interaction_ids if i not in interaction_log]
        if missing:
            return False, f"Interaction IDs not found in log: {missing}"
        not_attributable = [
            i for i in self.interaction_ids
            if not interaction_log[i].is_attributable()
        ]
        if not_attributable:
            return False, f"Interactions not attributable (logged late or no outcome note): {not_attributable}"
        quantified = any([
            self.hours_saved_per_month,
            self.use_case_id,
            self.error_rate_before is not None and self.error_rate_after is not None,
        ])
        if not quantified:
            return False, "No quantification provided — add hours_saved, use_case_id, or error rates"
        return True, "OK"

    def executive_summary_line(self) -> str:
        if self.outcome_type == "time_saving" and self.hours_saved_per_month:
            annual = self.hours_saved_per_month * 12
            return (f"{self.peer_name} ({self.outcome_type}): "
                    f"{self.hours_saved_per_month:.0f}h/month saved "
                    f"({annual:.0f}h/year) via champion {self.champion_name}")
        if self.outcome_type == "use_case_deployed":
            return (f"Use case {self.use_case_id} deployed with documented champion "
                    f"involvement from {self.champion_name}")
        return f"{self.peer_name}: {self.description}"

outcome = ChampionBusinessOutcome(
    outcome_id="OUT-2025-Q2-003",
    outcome_type="time_saving",
    peer_name="Alex Patel",
    champion_name="Jordan Lee",
    interaction_ids=["INT-2025-0412-001"],
    description="Contract first-pass review time reduced from 60 to 15 minutes per contract",
    hours_saved_per_month=45 * 3 / 60,  # 3 contracts/week, 45 min each
)
print(outcome.executive_summary_line())
# Alex Patel (time_saving): 2h/month saved (27h/year) via champion Jordan Lee
The Quarterly Impact Report
Each quarter the CoP lead compiles a champion network impact report from the interaction log and follow-up survey data. The report has four sections:
Section
Content
Audience
Network activity summary
Reach count, interaction volume by type, champion activity distribution (which champions are active; which are inactive)
CoP Lead, Working Group Leads
Engagement and skill change
Engagement rate from 30-day follow-up, skill change count and breakdown by BU and role
Programme Steering Committee
Behaviour change highlights
Count of peers with embedded adoption; three narrative case studies (one per BU where available)
Executive Sponsor
Business outcomes register
All verified outcome entries from the quarter; headline metrics (total hours saved, use cases with champion involvement)
Executive Sponsor, Finance/CFO where ROI is required

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class QuarterlyImpactReport:
    period: str
    # Funnel counts
    reach: int
    engaged: int
    skill_changed: int
    behaviour_changed: int
    business_outcomes: int
    # Activity
    total_interactions: int
    active_champions: int
    total_champions: int
    # Outcomes
    total_hours_saved_per_month: float
    use_cases_with_champion_involvement: int

    def engagement_rate(self) -> float:
        return self.engaged / max(self.reach, 1) * 100

    def funnel_conversion(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "reach_to_engaged":         f"{self.engagement_rate():.0f}%",
            "engaged_to_skill_change":  f"{self.skill_changed / max(self.engaged, 1) * 100:.0f}%",
            "skill_to_behaviour":       f"{self.behaviour_changed / max(self.skill_changed, 1) * 100:.0f}%",
            "behaviour_to_outcome_pct": f"{self.business_outcomes / max(self.behaviour_changed, 1) * 100:.0f}%",
        }

    def executive_summary(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"Quarter: {self.period}",
            f"Champion network reached {self.reach} peers across the organisation.",
            f"  {self.engaged} ({self.engagement_rate():.0f}%) applied a champion recommendation.",
            f"  {self.skill_changed} demonstrated measurable skill improvement.",
            f"  {self.behaviour_changed} have embedded GenAI tools in daily workflow.",
            f"  {self.business_outcomes} use cases or time-saving outcomes verified.",
            f"Time saved (verified, annualised): {self.total_hours_saved_per_month * 12:.0f} hours.",
            f"Active champions: {self.active_champions}/{self.total_champions}.",
        ]
        return "\n".join(lines)

report = QuarterlyImpactReport(
    period="2025-Q2",
    reach=1240, engaged=744, skill_changed=372,
    behaviour_changed=186, business_outcomes=14,
    total_interactions=890,
    active_champions=18, total_champions=22,
    total_hours_saved_per_month=320,
    use_cases_with_champion_involvement=8,
)
print(report.executive_summary())
print("Funnel:", report.funnel_conversion())
Common Attribution Errors
Error 1: Retroactive Attribution

The most common error: a use case is deployed, someone remembers that the team attended a champion office hours session six months ago, and the session is retroactively cited as champion involvement. This is not attributable under a defensible model — the interaction log must contain a contemporaneous entry.

Prevention: train champions that attribution requires a log entry within 24 hours. Any outcome claimed without a log entry is not counted in the quarterly report, regardless of how convincing the anecdote is.
Error 2: Counting Non-Champion Interactions

A peer who attended a mandatory GenAI training course and then adopted a tool is not a champion network outcome — the training programme gets that credit. The attribution model must track which peers entered the funnel via a
champion interaction specifically
, not via any GenAI programme touchpoint.
Error 3: Conflating Reach with Impact

An executive sponsor who hears '1,240 peers reached' and does not hear the funnel conversion rates will assume 1,240 peers adopted GenAI. Make the funnel visible in every report so the reach figure is always contextualised by its downstream conversion.
Error 4: Ignoring Inactive Champions in the Denominator
If 22 champions are enrolled but only 18 are active, the impact report should reflect
22 champions

in the network header and 18 active ones — not just 18. Hiding inactive champions makes the network appear healthier than it is and prevents the capacity and off-ramp conversations that the inactive champions need.
Summary
Champion network impact tracking uses a five-layer funnel:
Reach
(who had a champion interaction),
Engagement
(who acted on it within 30 days),
Skill Change
(who demonstrably improved),
Behaviour Change
(who embedded GenAI in daily work), and
Business Outcome
(what verifiable value was created). Each layer requires a different instrument — interaction logs, 30-day follow-up pulses, skill assessments, and the business outcomes register.

Attribution is defensible when interactions are logged within 24 hours with a concrete outcome note, and when business outcomes reference specific log entries that were written at the time of the interaction. The quarterly impact report compiles all four sections for different audiences, making the network's value visible to the executive sponsor without overclaiming. Common attribution errors — retroactive claiming, conflating reach with impact, hiding inactive champions — erode the model's credibility if not actively prevented.
